The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]

{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[Shintō shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[Ise-jingū]] ([[Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[Ōsaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}
==References== {{reflist}}

The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[Ise-jingū]] ([[Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[Ōsaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>

These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />

After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />

These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[Shintō shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[Ise-jingū]] ([[Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[Ōsaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

[[sn:Sengū]] [[en:Sengū]]

{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[Shintō shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[Ise-jingū]] ([[Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[Ōsaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Shrine|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Shrine|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

[[sn:Sengū]] [[en:Sengū]]

revision.comment: Bot: mark page as missing translated-page template
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[Shintō shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[Ise-jingū]] ([[Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[Ōsaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links ==
* Nakanishi Masayuki: „[https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 Shikinensengū]“. In: Encyclopedia of Shinto. Kokugaku-in, 11. November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]
{{Improve categories|date=November 2023}}]] ]]

The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />

One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>

In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

[[sn:Sengū]] [[en:Sengū]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sengū}} [[de:Sengū]] [[ja:遷宮]]

{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[Shintō shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[Ise-jingū]] ([[Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[Ōsaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links ==
* Nakanishi Masayuki: „[https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 Shikinensengū]“. In: Encyclopedia of Shinto. Kokugaku-in, 11. November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]
{{Improve categories|date=November 2023}}]] ]]

After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[Shintō shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ([[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) and [[Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}
==References== {{reflist}}

* Nakanishi Masayuki: „[https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 Shikinensengū]“. In: Encyclopedia of Shinto. Kokugaku-in, 11. November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]
{{Improve categories|date=November 2023}}]] ]]

The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ([[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2271023]] Wikidata name: sengū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ([[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links ==
* Nakanishi Masayuki: „[https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 Shikinensengū]“. In: Encyclopedia of Shinto. Kokugaku-in, 11. November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]

The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ([[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>

It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2271023]] Wikidata name: sengū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ([[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]] in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}

November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]

It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingu|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingu|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingū|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingū|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2271023]] Wikidata name: sengū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingū|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingū|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />

November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]
{{Improve categories|date=November 2023}}]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sengū]][[de:Sengū]][[en:Sengū]][[ja:遷宮]]
{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingū|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingū|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links ==
* Nakanishi Masayuki: „[https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 Shikinensengū]“. In: Encyclopedia of Shinto. Kokugaku-in, 11. November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]
{{Improve categories|date=November 2023}}]]

{{Short description|Shinto practice of rebuilding shrines}} {{nihongo|'''Sengū'''|遷宮}} is the Japanese term for the transfer of a [[shintai]] to a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313203709/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9063 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Shikinensengū | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref> When the [[honden]], the main hall of a Shintō shrine, is repaired or rebuilt, the shintai must be moved. Ceremonies to mark this occasion are also called Sengū.<ref name=":0" /> During sengū, the shrine's main buildings and sacred items are rebuilt and renewed. The main sanctuary and other structures are constructed anew. All sacred garments and treasures are also replaced.<ref name=":0" />
Jingū Shikinen Sengū (神宮式年遷宮, [[:ja:神宮式年遷宮|神宮式年遷宮]]) is the term used for ceremonies at [[:en:Ise-jingū|Ise-jingū]] ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}) and [[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} in which the main hall is identically and completely rebuilt at fixed intervals (shikinen) on one of two adjacent sites. At the shrine renewal, the shintai is then transferred to the new honden at a solemn ceremony (sengū) and the old building is burned down. One of the new sacred mirrors is offered at this time by the [[:en:Tennō|Tennō]] himself. It is said that this ceremony is related to the Shikinensai, the commemoration of the anniversary of the death of a historical emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.today/20230313202939/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8702 |access-date=2023-03-13 |website=archive.ph |title=Saishu | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-02-12 |title=Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests |url=http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |access-date=2023-03-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html |archive-date=2011-02-12 }}</ref>
The sengū ritual began in the 7th century. It was initiated by Emperor Tenmu during a time of civil unrest. The first sengū ceremony took place in 690 for the Inner Shrine and in 692 for the Outer Shrine. This tradition has continued for over 1,300 years.<ref name=":0" />
The process of rebuilding the shrine is governed by specific laws. These were first recorded in the Engi period (901–922). The laws state that every twenty years, the main sanctuary and other buildings must be renewed. All materials used must be new. The sacred treasures are then moved to the new buildings.<ref name=":0" />
The shrine’s design is simple yet distinctive. It uses materials like cypress wood, miscanthus reeds, and metal hardware. The main sanctuary has a special central post. The simple design means the buildings decay over time, requiring periodic renewal.<ref name=":0" />
The renewal process is a large-scale project. It takes eight years to complete. It requires thousands of pieces of timber, reeds, and many carpenters. Over sixty structures are rebuilt. These include sanctuaries, treasure houses, and gates. New sacred garments and treasures are also made.<ref name=":0" />
There are various ceremonies during the construction. These include rituals for cutting and transporting wood and building the shrine. The main ritual, Sengyo, happens in October. It marks the transfer of the deity to the new shrine. During this time, the emperor performs a distant worship ritual.<ref name=":0" />
The sengū ritual has evolved over time. In the past, the imperial court managed the rebuilding. Later, a rice tax was used to fund the construction. After a break in the tradition due to warfare, the ritual resumed in the 16th century. In the modern era, the Japanese government oversees the process.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to the shrines at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi]], such ceremonies were once held at other shrines, including the [[Katori Jingū|Katori-jingū]], the [[Kashima Jingū|Kashima-jingū]], the [[Usa Jingū|Usa-jingū]], the [[Kasuga-taisha]], and the [[Suwa-taisha]] (the Onbashira-sai).<ref name=":0" />
==See also == * {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}

November 2006 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110212071852/http://int.kateigaho.com/spr05/forests-ise.html "Ise Shine - Japan's Sacred Forests"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150313235623/https://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/sikinen/sikinen.htm The Shikinen Sengu ceremony in Ise Jingu]
